headlines.title: ARGUMENTS BEGIN IN TRIAL OF MRS. REUTER
headlines.subtitle: Accused Woman Collapsed Yesterday, but Recovered at Tuesday Session.
headlines.fullText: By traurigted Press. BARTLESVILLE, Okla, Jan. 27 .- The second trial of Mlrs. Laura M. Rester drew into its final chapter this morning when Judge Rt. C. Allen read his instructions to the jury and Nat Ligon, assistant prosecuting attorney of Tulsa county, delivered the state's theory and made a powerful plea for conviction on the first ballot. Not overlooking a single fact of circumstance In the case, Ligon, in an address to the jury, took up nearly three hours summing up. On Mrs. Reuter's action on the day following the killing of her husband he laid special stress. The case will probably be closed shortly. Mrs. Reuter aald: "I feel het- ter today and slept better last night than I have for many weeks." Mrs. Reuter collapsed twice in the court room yesterday, but appeared en- tirely recovered tubes the defense. Ligon will be followed by two at Ed Crossland will then talk for the state and J It. Charlton and Pat Sfal- Iny will make the final arguments on be no night sessions during arguments, it is likely that the case will go to the jury late Thursday

headlines.title: LOICANO REARRESTED ON APPEALS DECISION
headlines.fullText: A. Loicano, convicted in the county criminal court last Oct. 17 tor selling Mauer without a license, was re- arrested Tuesday morning by Police- men Kreyenbuhl and Dollins and will be delivered to county officers. Laicano was fined $250 and sen- teneed to one day in jail. The case was appealed and Loicano made bond pending the decision of the court of firmed last Wednesday but the de- fondant was not located until Tuesday He was arrested at Greet's restau- rant, 1603 East Front street.

headlines.title: Resolutions of Respect.
headlines.subtitle: To the Memory ar Capcom the First National Bank of Mount Pleasant. Jan. 13. 1914:
headlines.fullText: We. the shareholders of the First We, National Bank of Mount Pleasant. Texas in regular ma! meeting held in the banking house, in Mount Pleasant, this, the fath day of Janu- ary. 1914, by unanimous vote wish to :Sedeen regret at the death on January 12 1914. of our honored friend and associate for many years. Captain M. L., Cotter. We further wish to offer our earnest and profound sympathy to his beloved son and daughter. Mr. and Mrs. John J. Denman. It is further resolved that a copy of this resolution be spread on the minutes of this, our annual meet- Fort Worth, Texas, and that a 095 ?.. this, perolution be published in the J. B. TENNISON. IL. H. HAYDON. Committee.

headlines.title: B'NAI B'RITH MEMBERS GO TO DALLAS TONIGHT
headlines.subtitle: Special Car Will Carry Fort Worth Members to Celebration
headlines.fullText: A special cat for fout writh to the Dallas celebration of H'nal IFrith day will leave the station at Third anna eration will be welcomed to use the special accommodations. Leon Schwarts of Mobile, Ala. ex- dent of this district in the B'nat Trrith order. will be the principal speaker at the Dallas convention.

headlines.title: DEATHS
headlines.fullText: Hart-The funeral of Mrs. Joan Hart, 78. who died Monday at Hand- est be held wednesday morning 's o'clock from the residence in Haber Park of St. Catholic Church conducting the services. Burial will follow in Calvary cemetery.

headlines.title: IN MEMORIAM.
headlines.fullText: "After Life's fitful fever he siceps This community, as well as every district in which Captain M. L. Cotter Is known, was deeply grieved.to learn of his death at his home in port Worth, Texas, after a long and paintul While a citizen of Mt. Pleasant he won the esteem and confidence of all who knew him. He was a man of a rare combination of qualities, gentle- ness, with force of character, prudence trustworthy fry trustworthfly every relation which he megof which saturally fell to fils der and self-sacrificing adevoten mother. A friend unfailing in Jovatty faithfulness and helpfulness upright and just. A careful "na sure crastul man of business co mens would almost count onism little children toved him and recom- nized in him a friend, and many young men and women, now grown, recall with pleasure their journey nes from Mt. Pleasant to Fort Worth under the Fare of Captain Cotter. All who knew hin tender care of his own little daugh- ter felt safe to trust their little ones to Only the physical body of our hon- ered friend will be missed from our and into Immer- tality and ever will be felt; the uplift- example, his clean life, his never falling kindness and his intolerance of mean and ig- noble conduet. By all who knew him he was pro- foundly respected and beloved. It is known as -a gond wanm 19 universally ANNIE M. TOWLER.
